text:
Hey all. Introductions? Ok, well, I made video games for 25+ years C64->PS3, worked on the Chrome GPU team at Google making WebGL, and stumbled on the Future of Coding podcast recently.

I don't know if have anything to add but I am interested in the topic. I wrote a simple signal based visual programming system for a game I worked on. I worked with a visual programming system called "Virtools" in like 2004. I also got lucky and by chance got to visit Dynamicland last March. I would love to discuss all that stuff somewhere and learn more.
Let’s do 1pm. I put it in my calendar. The Teishoku place sounds good to me, or we can go to Savoy. Let’s play it by ear when I’m there.
Not quite the same idea, but there have been a few "Autocomplete from Stack Overflow" projects. Here's one: <https://emilschutte.com/stackoverflow-autocomplete/>
Not quite the same idea, but there have been a few "Autocomplete from Stack Overflow" projects. Here's one: <https://emilschutte.com/stackoverflow-autocomplete/>

It feels like your idea is almost the reverse, though. I imagine a programming tool where you write unit tests, submit them to Mechanical Turk, and get back code that satisfies the tests. I wonder how [in]efficient that'd be, in an economic sense. (I wouldn't be at all surprised if there has already been an ICO for a service that does something like this.)

It feels like your idea is almost the reverse, though. I imagine a programming tool where you write unit tests, submit them to Mechanical Turk, and get back code that satisfies the tests. I wonder how [in]efficient that'd be, in an economic sense. (I wouldn't be at all surprised if there has already been an ICO for a service that does something like this.)

I've found it most essential to have a reasonably well-bounded end state. You can't "win" if you don't know what winning is. Whether it's some idea that I want reify (or find out of someone else has already realized it), or it's a particular pain that my company (or society/civilization) is feeling that I'd like to remove, or something I want to learn just to keep abreast of technology and our culture, I think it's key to define a goal condition for your research-like activity, as specifically as you can manage.

A really helpful yardstick I've found when approaching learning material (papers, blog posts, talks, books, etc) is to determine if something is or isn't going to be *pabulum* — intellectual, thought-provoking, informative, but ultimately bland and _entertaining_ to me, rather than _helpful_ to me. This is a very difficult determination to make in the absence of a specific goal.

I also find it's valuable to do whatever I can to make sure I'm highly excited about what I'm working on. That might be a luxury not everyone can afford, but I think we're all here in this Slack because of some measure of mutual passion, so I think it's safe to take this value as a given here. Enthusiasm is what determines how I structure my time at a fine-grained level — "What small step toward my end goal am I most excited about taking next?" The pabulum filter + specific end goal keeps me from treading water, while this "chase your curiosity" approach keeps the work from becoming monotonous. One day I might make a paper prototype. Then I might decide that paper lacks "liveness", so I grab a stop-motion app and make an animated paper prototype. Then I might decide that I really need to interact with the system, so I ask my wife to help me "act out" the HCI, moving the paper pieces around according to some ruleset. After refining the ruleset, I have something structured enough to prototype in Keynote or hypertext. In each of these steps, I'm doing work that I need to do, but in a way that keeps me away from writing code, since today I'm less excited about writing code than I am about (say) scissors.

That's my recipe.
* Be excited to go somewhere.
* Be sure you're always moving in that direction.
* And be absolutely sure there's a "there" there.

At around 52 minutes, he starts hammering on "Conveyance" and I almost did a spit-take. One of the ways I've been thinking about my current project is as a "conveyance-oriented programming language". Unfortunately (or thankfully?), the meaning of conveyance he's using is slightly different from the one I'm using, so I don't think they'll be any toe-stepping going on here.

Regardless, this is an awesome talk.
